1.description: Financial mistake frequency. This figure plots the annual frequency of financial mistakes. Panel A shows the frequency of incurring a late fee. Panel B shows the frequency of incurring an overdraft fee. Panel C shows the frequency of incurring either type of fee. The bars are sorted by average number of yearly mistakes.

2.description: Consumption expenditure around HELOC reset. This figure plots the results from the event-study model with individual fixed effects where the outcome variable is monthly consumption expenditure. Panel A plots the coefficients on the vector of month dummies relative to the HELOC reset date when the regression is run for the full sample period. Panel B plots the same coefficients when the regression is run for the group of individuals who have made no financial mistakes (Group 0, solid line) and for the quartile of individuals who have made the most financial mistakes (Group 4, dotted line). The gray bars represent 95% confidence intervals of the point estimates based on standard errors clustered at the state-year level. Precise point estimates and standard errors are reported in TableIA.IVin theInternet Appendix.

3.description: Luxury Engel curves. This figure plots the average spending ratio on luxury goods relative to annual income for financially sophisticated individuals (individuals in Group 0, who never incurred avoidable card fees: black circles and solid line) and financially unsophisticated individuals (individuals in Group 4, the quartile of individuals who most frequently incurred avoidable card fees: white triangles and dotted line). Luxury expenditure is defined as the percentage of total expenditures that is spent on entertainment, electronics, jewelry and arts, retail, and travel. The dots represent 33 equal-sized bins based on annual income, and the lines are local linear regressions on the entire data set. See SectionIII.Afor details on sample construction and SectionIII.Bfor details on the construction of luxury expenditures.
